headline: Ben Erez on analytical thinking interviews: stop hunting for the right answer, start generating the right signals
digest_md:
Ben Erez — decade in product at Facebook, first PM at three startups, full-time PM-interview coach — argues that most candidates fail analytical thinking (AT) interviews not because their answer is wrong, but because they treat the interview as a casual conversation about a product instead of a structured game where the interviewer is trying to capture specific signals.

The AT interview's job: assess your ability to *'understand a product in the context of its broader company and its market, establish metrics to track success, identify team goals, and evaluate tradeoffs in a structured way.'* Typical questions: *'How would you measure success for Spotify?'* / *'Set a goal for Instagram Reels.'* / *'What should be the North Star metric for DoorDash?'*

Erez's framework — what to do, in order, in the 35 minutes you actually have:

1. **Assumptions and game plan (first minute).** State a handful of assumptions that narrow scope without limiting the solution space. Then explicitly outline how you'll spend the time: *'I want to start by reviewing the product's landscape and reason for existing, then identify key stakeholders and ecosystem health metrics, define a North Star metric with guardrails, and finally set specific team goals.'*
2. **Linear flow.** Product rationale → stakeholders + ecosystem metrics → North Star + guardrails → team goals. Tradeoffs threaded throughout.
3. **Transition check-ins.** At each section boundary, pause, gather your thoughts, and confirm the interviewer is following before proceeding.
4. **Inner time cop.** 45-minute slot, 35 minutes of working time. Run your own clock.

The central reframe: *'the interviewer won't be looking for a 'correct' answer — rather, they're evaluating your thought process.'* Most candidates jump to goals or metrics. The signal is in the structure, the assumptions, and the game plan.

For a mid-level PM preparing for the next interview loop: this is a week-by-week practice. Drill the assumptions step. Memorize the linear flow. Run mocks where you deliberately announce the game plan in the first 60 seconds.
takeaways: Before your next mock or real AT interview, write your standard 4-point game-plan sentence in advance. Open every interview with it. The first 60 seconds is where most candidates lose the loop.
Practice the assumptions step in isolation. Take three random products this week and write three scope-narrowing assumptions for each in under 90 seconds. This is the muscle.
Stop trying to find the 'right' North Star. Define a North Star *with guardrails*. The guardrails are where senior PMs are graded.
Run a 35-minute timer in your next mock and force a transition check-in at minute 7, 17, 27. The signal interviewers want is structure, not depth on any one section.

source.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews

questions.scenario_md:
You have a Meta AT interview Thursday. You read three Lenny posts on metrics and feel ready. In your last mock with a peer, you jumped straight into 'I'd track DAU, retention, and time spent' the moment the prompt landed, and your peer said it felt rushed. You're considering doing more reading.

Per Erez, the missing step in your prep is —

options.text: Drill the first 60 seconds: write and rehearse a standard assumptions + game-plan opener so you start every interview as a structured game.
Read deeper case studies on how Meta sets goals internally.
Memorize 5-7 metric frameworks so you have one ready for any prompt.
Practice estimation questions; they're the new bottleneck.

questions.explanation_md: Erez's central frame is to approach the interview as a game with clear rules — you spend the first minute on assumptions and a game plan that tells the interviewer how you'll spend the time. Jumping straight to metrics is the failure mode candidates lose on; the prep gap is the opener, not the metric depth.
questions.pm_takeaway: Before your next mock, write a 60-second opener: 3 scope-narrowing assumptions + your linear-flow game plan. Read it aloud once before any mock starts.

citation.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews
citation.quote_excerpt: My advice is to approach the interview as a game with clear rules rather than a casual conversation, and spend the first minute making a handful of assumptions at the start of the interview that narrow the scope to a manageable exercise without prematurely limiting the solution space.

questions.scenario_md:
Your peer who interviewed at the same company reports back: she didn't pick the metric you were planning on, but she got a strong signal hire. You're now anxious about choosing the 'right' North Star metric for your interview Friday.

Per Erez, the most accurate read on your anxiety is —

options.text: She got lucky with a generous interviewer; pick the safest metric — DAU or revenue — to maximize your odds.
Different interviewers expect different metrics; ask your recruiter for examples of accepted answers.
Misplaced anxiety — the interviewer evaluates thought process, not metric choice; your structure and reasoning matter more than the metric label.
Different products have different correct metrics; spend the week mapping each company's right answer.

questions.explanation_md: Erez is explicit: the interviewer isn't looking for a correct answer — they're evaluating your thought process. The defense against metric anxiety is to invest the week in structured-reasoning practice (assumptions, linear flow, tradeoff articulation), not in memorizing 'the right' metric.
questions.pm_takeaway: Tomorrow, do a 35-minute mock where you deliberately pick a non-obvious North Star and defend it with structure. The grade is the structure, not the metric.

citation.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews
citation.quote_excerpt: the interviewer won’t be looking for a “correct” answer—rather, they’re evaluating your thought process

questions.scenario_md:
You're 18 minutes into a 35-minute mock for 'How would you measure success for Spotify?' You've stated assumptions, listed users and stakeholders, and proposed a North Star. Your peer-interviewer asks: 'What about ad-revenue? Should that be your North Star instead?' You feel the urge to pivot and reopen the metrics discussion.

Applying Erez's linear-flow framework, the right move is —

options.text: Pivot fully to ad-revenue framing; the interviewer is signaling preference.
Acknowledge the tradeoff aloud, note it as a guardrail consideration, and proceed to team goals as planned.
Defend your North Star, push back, and continue.
Restart the metrics section with ad-revenue as the new framing.

questions.explanation_md: The linear flow goes product rationale → stakeholders → North Star + guardrails → team goals, with tradeoffs threaded throughout. The senior move is to surface the tradeoff explicitly without abandoning the structure or restarting — which is what generates the signal of structured thinking.
questions.pm_takeaway: In your next mock, pre-decide that any pushback gets one explicit tradeoff acknowledgment, then you proceed. Do not restart sections.

citation.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews
citation.quote_excerpt: I want to start by reviewing the product’s landscape and reason for existing, then identify key stakeholders and ecosystem health metrics, define a North Star metric with guardrails, and finally set specific team goals. We can discuss tradeoffs along the way at any point.

questions.scenario_md:
Prompt: 'What should be the North Star metric for DoorDash?' You have 60 seconds before you start. Four candidate opening assumption-sets are on the wall:

1. 'I'll assume we're focusing on the U.S. market and all platforms; the marketplace covers customers, restaurants, and dashers.'
2. 'I'll assume DoorDash is profitable and we're optimizing for shareholder return.'
3. 'I'll assume the question is hypothetical and pick whatever metric I like.'
4. 'I'll assume we're at the IPO stage and need to maximize valuation multiples.'

Per Erez, the right opener is —

options.text: Option 2 — anchors on shareholder return as the most senior framing.
Option 4 — IPO framing tells the interviewer you think strategically.
Option 3 — keeps it loose; the interviewer values flexibility.
Option 1 — names the marketplace, narrows geography and platforms, leaves the metric solution space open.

questions.explanation_md: Erez's assumptions framework is to narrow scope without prematurely limiting the solution space. Option 1 names the three-sided marketplace (customers, restaurants, dashers), narrows region and platforms, and leaves the metric choice fully open — exactly the signal interviewers want.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week, drill three random products with the assumptions step alone — region, platforms, ecosystem participants — under 90 seconds each. Do not attempt the metric.

citation.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews
citation.quote_excerpt: mention customers, restaurants, and dashers up front, signaling that you think about the entire marketplace.   - Focusing on the U.S. market prevents overwhelming complexity, and including all platforms/devices keeps the exercise comprehensive.

questions.scenario_md:
Mock prompt: 'Set a goal for Instagram Reels.' Your draft answer: 'Daily active Reels users, target 20% YoY growth.' Your peer-interviewer asks for 'tradeoffs and guardrails.' You blank.

The response that best translates Erez's North-Star-plus-guardrails framing is —

options.text: 'I'd lower the target to 15% to reduce risk.'
'My guardrails would be session quality (e.g. completion rate) and creator earnings — DAU growth that drops either is gamed, not real.'
'I'd add weekly active users as a confirmation metric.'
'I'd swap to MAU; it's more stable.'

questions.explanation_md: Erez's North Star framing comes paired with guardrails — metrics that prevent the goal from being gamed at the expense of ecosystem health. The senior translation names *what* the guardrails protect (quality, creator earnings) and *why*: any North Star lift that violates a guardrail isn't a real win.
questions.pm_takeaway: Before your next mock, pre-write your guardrail vocabulary for 5 product types (marketplace, social, B2B SaaS, consumer subscription, ad-supported) so guardrails feel automatic in the moment.

citation.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews
citation.quote_excerpt: define a North Star metric with guardrails, and finally set specific team goals
